intro.kicker: Topic 4 of 10 • Chapter: Newton's Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: If no external force acts on a system (called isolated) of constant mass, the total momentum of the system remains constant with time. Conservation of linear momentum is equivalent to Newton's third law of motion.

difficulty.description: High conceptual clarity required on multi-body systems

bulletNotes: Crucial topic for collisions and recoil mechanisms.
Often integrated with Work, Energy, and Power.

strategySteps.title: Master the Basics
strategySteps.description: Ensure understanding of internal vs external forces.

strategySteps.title: Practice Recoil
strategySteps.description: Solve gun-bullet and bomb explosion numericals rigorously.

cards.title: Complete Topic Notes
cards.description: Comprehensive PDF with all derivations for isolated systems.

cards.title: The Core Principle
cards.coreRevision: When the net external force on a system is zero, its total linear momentum remains conserved.
cards.example: A bomb exploding in mid-air distributes its momentum strictly such that the vector sum of all fragment momenta equals the original momentum.

cards.title: Gun Recoil
cards.coreRevision: The recoil velocity of a gun fired horizontally is derived directly from the conservation of momentum.
points: The minus sign means the gun recoils opposite to the bullet velocity.
cards.example: If a 5 kg gun fires a 10 g bullet at 500 m/s, the gun recoils at 1 m/s backwards.

curriculum.hintText: Common gaps between state boards and NEET structure.

gaps.title: Multi-Dimensional Collisions
gaps.description: Some curriculum focuses only on 1D collisions.
bullets: Ensure practice in 2D vector resolutions for momentum.

questions.question: A heavy bowling ball transfers momentum to a stationary football. The total linear momentum of the system is:
questions.explanation: By the law of conservation of linear momentum, in the absence of external forces, total momentum remains conserved.

questions.question: A 10g bullet is fired from a 5kg gun with velocity 400 m/s. What is the recoil velocity?

items.title: Understand isolated systems
items.description: Identify what constitutes an internal versus external force.

items.question: Does momentum conserve in inelastic collisions?
items.answer: Yes, linear momentum is conserved in all collisions, whether elastic or inelastic, as long as external forces are zero.

grounding_summary: Conservation of linear momentum holds when external force is zero.
Recoil of gun and rocket propulsion are key applications derived from Newton's laws.
grounding_excerpt: Here negative sign indicates that the velocity of recoil
By the law of conservation of linear momentum
